comment_content:
Just watched it . The story felt confusing and rushed, with too many new ideas and not enough focus on the characters we care about.
My biggest letdown was how it treated Joe and Nicky. In the first movie, their love was powerful and moving. In this one, they barely get any screen time together, and their relationship is almost completely ignored. It feels like the heart of the story is missing.
Overall, the sequel focuses too much on action and forgets what made the original so special.This was really dissappointing
reply_content: I agree. I didn’t love everything about the first movie, but at least it allowed the audience to buy into the idea that the characters are hundreds and thousands of years old. In this one, they’re just portrayed as a band of mercenaries, with a bunch of half-cooked ideas sprinkled over them. Uma Thurman’s character is paper-thin and doesn’t seem powerful or threatening: it’s not enough to say “uh, she’s something”, you have to show it.

comment_content:
Charlize delivers, the rest needs to catch up
The Old Guard 2 understood the value of its leading lady. Seeing Charlize Theron in every action scene, unobscured by stunts or cheap tricks, is absolutely the film's strong point, setting it apart from other generic and forgettable productions. Charlize is convincing as an immortal warrior because her body is on display—kicking, punching, fighting with the precision and elegance of a true martial artist. The choreography has also evolved, abandoning (thankfully!) the exhaustively recycled "John Wick-style" formula, in which the character hits furniture more than enemies. In Old Guard 2, we see striking, high kicks, and hand-to-hand combat that excites because it finally feels like fighting—not simulations edited to look like choreography. But not everything survived the editing room unscathed. The choice to speed up the footage and exaggerate the cuts weakens precisely the film's most valuable asset: the actress's physique and the credibility of the action. The excessive cuts are reminiscent of generic Marvel scenes, where no one knows who's fighting, where the blow came from, or why the camera is turning. If there's a path for the next sequence, it's clear: keep Charlize in focus, without unnecessary cuts. Preserve the natural speed of her movements—as you did masterfully in Atomic Blonde—and let her talent breathe. The audience will notice, and be grateful. You have a star capable of delivering real action, and a universe where that can shine. Just don't sabotage it with impatient editing or formulas from other films. If you're bold enough, The Old Guard could become a benchmark—and not just another forgettable franchise.
reply_content: What nonsense. Charlise was terrible in this. She looked like a grumpy, sullen teenager that walks as if she’s on the catwalk of a fashion show.

comment_content: very disappointed in this sequel. As someone who was a HUGE fan of the first one, I was quite surprised at how poorly this was written and made. It was not emotionally engaging, there was no real hook, and there was no real central story. Just a brief touch on many arcs at the same time. It made it impossible to feel anything for any of the arcs. I am genuinely disappointed in this sequel. So much so that this is the first time in my life I am ever leaving a review for a movie. Creating is not an easy task, especially when you've set a high bar for yourself, so i'll give those behind the movie kudos. But for a movie that I can sum up all the arcs in three sentences, I think this was a terrible sequel. So much wasted potential.
